content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 22, 2017

Daniel G. Sodergren
City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
P. O. Box 520
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-101

Dear Mr. Sodergren:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Planning Commissioner Nancy Allen participate in and vote on governmental decisions relating to the Downtown Specific Plan update (the "Plan") despite having an interest in her jewelry making business, and receiving income for the sale of jewelry from Studio Seven Arts, a business that leases retail space in the Plan area?

CONCLUSION

Yes. There may be minimal indirect impacts on Studio Seven Arts' use of the rented property resulting from decisions related to the Plan and there is no indication that any of the decisions will affect her jewelry making business or Studio Seven Arts to such an extent that it will change the value of either privately-held business entity. Therefore, Commissioner Allen does not have a conflict of interest in the decisions.

_________________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-101
Page No. 2

FACTS

Commissioner Allen is the sole proprietor of a business which makes jewelry. As reported
on her statement of economic interests, her business has annual gross revenue between $10,001 and
$100,000. According to Commissioner Allen's business website, her work is sold through several
galleries and retail establishments in Northern California, as well as at art festivals.

In Pleasanton, Commissioner Allen's jewelry is sold by Studio Seven Arts.² Studio Seven
Arts rents space at 400 Main Street, Pleasanton, in the Downtown Specific Plan area. Sales of
Commissioner Allen's jewelry at Studio Seven Arts are conducted on a consignment basis; she is
not an employee and has no ownership interest. In the prior 12 months, gross revenue from Studio
Seven Arts to Commissioner Allen has been about $2,000.³ Commissioner Allen also notes that this
gross revenue from Studio Seven Arts is less than 1 % of her annual gross income, and less than
0.25% of her net income. Studio Seven Arts advises that its consignment sales of Commissioner
Allen's jewelry represent less than 0.5% of its annual store sales. You also note that Studio Seven
Arts is not a formal applicant before the City, but rather its location is within the Downtown
Specific Plan area.

The City's current Downtown Specific Plan was adopted in 2002. The Plan is intended to
serve as the primary regulatory guide for preserving and enhancing the 308-acre Downtown area.
The Plan contains policies, programs, and regulations for implementing the City's General Plan in
the Downtown area. This includes provisions relating to: land use; transportation; parking; public
facilities; historic preservation; design and beautification; and economic vitality. Under the state
Planning and Zoning Law, no City public works project may be approved, no tentative or parcel
map be approved, and no zoning ordinance amendment may be adopted within the Plan area unless
it is consistent with the Plan. (Government Code, § 65455.) In a May 8, 2017 voicemail message,
you stated that Studio Seven Arts, which is located within the Plan area, previously had a long-term
lease on the property, and was currently in a month-to-month tenancy while in the process of
renegotiating another long-term lease.

The City is preparing to comprehensively update the Plan. This process will include the
formation of a Downtown Specific Plan Task Force, which will issue recommendations to the
City's Planning Commission and City Council on amendments to the Plan, the City's General
Plan, and Zoning Ordinance, described as follows:

[R]evaluating certain elements of the [Downtown Specific] plan to:

• Clarify areas of policy direction that are vague,
• More effectively respond to market factors and community objectives,

² Your request notes that Studio Seven Arts requires exclusivity; thus, Commissioner Allen's jewelry on sale
through Studio Seven Arts is not sold by other local retailers

³ "Consignment" is a term that can encompass various sales arrangements. The question of who is considered
the source of income will turn on whether the individuals who are the consignees are the actual buyers of the items, or
whether they are selling the items as agents of the official's business, with the official's business retaining ownership.
As your request states that Studio Seven Arts is a source of income to Commissioner Allen, we are treating it as such
for this analysis.

File No. A-17-101
Page No. 3

• Strengthen the protection of physical features of Downtown that are valued by the community, and
• Incorporate key concepts and principles from recent long-term planning efforts.

Initially, Plan topics that were targeted for an update or clarification included: integration of residential development, public parking, and the Office land use district. After receiving informal feedback from the community, this list expanded to include potential topics for consideration such as: parking requirements for development projects; second units (or accessory dwelling units); maximum building height in Downtown for residential and commercial properties; clarification of maximum building height for residential and commercial properties; trail/pathway integration; enhancement of Division Street; integration of recent long term planning efforts (e.g., Civic Center Master Plan); and green design features.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87103.) Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association. You have stated that Commissioner Allen operates a jewelry making business, with annual gross revenue between $10,001 and $100,000. You have also stated that she receives income from Studio Seven Arts.

Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Under your facts, Commissioner Allen's jewelry business and Studio Seven Arts are not explicitly involved in the Plan. Thus, as applied to the jewelry business and Studio Seven Arts, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

File No. A-17-101
Page No. 4


Materiality: Interest in a Business Entity and Business Entity as Source of Income

The standard to determine materiality for a business entity, and business entity as source of income,⁴ is set forth in Regulation 18702.1(b) (business entity - not explicitly involved). Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change . . . in the value of a . . . privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.1(b).) The regulation provides various examples of the types of decisions that may impact the analysis such as those that:

• Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged.

• Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged.

• Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies.

• Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives.

• Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project.

• Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business entity.

There is no indication that the Plan will affect the value of Commissioner Allen's jewelry making business. Studio Seven Arts is in the process of renegotiating another long-term lease, and there is no indication that the Plan would affect Studio Seven Arts' lease agreement. Moreover, the Plan will not affect the actual use of the property that Studio Seven Arts occupies, or the amount of business it receives. While there may be minimal indirect impacts on Studio Seven Arts' use of the property resulting from decisions made related to the Plan, there is no indication that any of the decisions are of magnitude that will affect the value of Studio Seven Arts. Therefore, Commissioner Allen does not have a conflict of interest in the decision.

___________________________
⁴ See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4). Where the source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers back to the standard of applied in 18702.1 for business entities.

File No. A-17-101
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:    Zachary W. Norton
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, 
syndicate, corporation or association. You have stated that Commissioner Allen operates a jewelry 
making business, with annual gross revenue between $10,00 I and $100,000. You have also stated 
that she receives income from Studio Seven Arts.

The standard to detennine materiality for a business entity, and business entity as source of
